title: UAE's Sheikh Mohammed launches initiative to eradicate blindness in Emiratis
summary: Programme design targets full eradication of river blindness by 2030 in supported locations
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
Dubai launched a three-year river blindness campaign targeting seven million direct beneficiaries and 35.4 million indirect beneficiaries across affected communities.

Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Vice President and Prime Minister of the UAE and Ruler of Dubai, announced the programme on 10 May in Dubai. Noor Dubai will deliver the work under Mohammed Bin Rashid Al Maktoum Global Initiatives.

Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Sheikh Maktoum bin Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Sheikh Ahmed bin Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum and senior health officials attended the launch.

Sheikh Mohammed said blindness prevention protects dignity and expands access to fuller lives.

Three-year delivery plan

Noor Dubai will oversee medicine distribution for prevention and treatment, community surveys, early disease assessments and local workforce training. Programme design targets full eradication of river blindness by 2030 in supported locations.

WHO identifies river blindness within its 2021 to 2030 road map for neglected tropical diseases. WHO also recognises 30 January as World Neglected Tropical Diseases Day after a World Health Assembly decision in 2021.

UAE diplomatic backing for the designation through the Reaching the Last Mile forum in Abu Dhabi in 2019.

Disease burden and field methods

River blindness, or onchocerciasis, affects the eyes and skin and can lead to permanent blindness. Severe itching, skin changes and eye lesions are among the main symptoms. Subcutaneous nodules in many cases and epilepsy risk from early exposure in children. Parasitic worms drive the disease burden.

WHO recorded more than 170 million ivermectin treatments in both 2023 and 2024, the highest annual totals to date. Dubai’s campaign enters that wider elimination cycle with medicine delivery, surveillance and trained local staff at its core.

Noor Dubai scales treatment capacity

Noor Dubai has improved the lives of more than 33 million beneficiaries across Asia and Africa by the end of 2024. Dubai Media Office records more than 3.8 million beneficiaries in 2024 alone. Dubai Media Office figures give the foundation an established treatment and prevention base for larger disease control campaigns.

Noor Dubai’s 2024 field operations included 18,279 medical examinations in the Philippines, 2,596 sight-saving surgeries in Nigeria, 4,846 medicines distributed in Pakistan and 3,095 prescription glasses in Bangladesh.

Noor Dubai’s iBSAR telemedicine project in Nepal delivered more than 13,176 virtual consultations.

Noor Dubai’s Mobile Eye Clinic treated 4,658 patients in the UAE in 2024 and has served more than 43,727 people since 2014. Local service capacity adds another operating channel as the foundation expands specialist eye care programmes.

Mohammed Bin Rashid Al Maktoum Global Initiatives has allocated more than AED13.8 billion to humanitarian aid and relief since 2015, benefiting 788 million people in 118 countries.

Dubai Media Office also records more than AED2.2 billion in spending and about 149 million beneficiaries in 2024 alone. MBRGI lists healthcare and disease control as one of its five operating pillars alongside education, innovation, community empowerment and humanitarian relief.
